**Club Brugge Koninklijke Voetbalvereniging** (Dutch pronunciation: [[klʏ ˈbrʏɣə ˌkoːnɪŋkləkə ˈvudbɑlvəˌreːnəɣɪŋ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch")),[[a]](#cite_note-2) known simply as **Club Brugge** (in English also: **Club Bruges**), is a Belgian professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges"), Belgium. It was founded in 1891 and its home ground is the [Jan Breydel Stadium](/wiki/Jan_Breydel_Stadium "Jan Breydel Stadium"), which has a capacity of 29,042.[[2]](#cite_note-stadiumguide.com-3) They play in the [Belgian Pro League](/wiki/Belgian_Pro_League "Belgian Pro League"), the top domestic league in Belgian football.

One of the most decorated clubs in [Belgian football](/wiki/Football_in_Belgium "Football in Belgium"), Club Brugge has been crowned [Belgian league champions](/wiki/List_of_Belgian_football_champions "List of Belgian football champions") 19 times, second only to [major rivals](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht%E2%80%93Club_Brugge_KV_rivalry "R.S.C. Anderlecht–Club Brugge KV rivalry") [Anderlecht](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht "R.S.C. Anderlecht"), and shares the [Jan Breydel Stadium](/wiki/Jan_Breydel_Stadium "Jan Breydel Stadium") with city rival [Cercle Brugge](/wiki/Cercle_Brugge_K.S.V. "Cercle Brugge K.S.V."), with whom they contest the [Bruges derby](/wiki/Bruges_derby "Bruges derby").[[3]](#cite_note-4)

Throughout its long history, "Club" has enjoyed much European football success, reaching two European finals and three European semi-finals. Club Brugge is the only Belgian club to have played the final of the European Cup (now the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League")) as of November 2024, losing to [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") in the final of the [1978](/wiki/1978_European_Cup_Final "1978 European Cup Final") season.[[4]](#cite_note-guardian-5) They also lost in the [1976 UEFA Cup Final](/wiki/1976_UEFA_Cup_Final "1976 UEFA Cup Final") to the same opponents.[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-mirror-7) Club Brugge holds the European record for the highest number of consecutive participations in the UEFA Europa League (20), the record number of [Belgian Cups](/wiki/Belgian_Cup "Belgian Cup") (12),[[7]](#cite_note-8) and the record number of [Belgian Super Cups](/wiki/Belgian_Super_Cup "Belgian Super Cup") (18).[[8]](#cite_note-9)

In 1890, students from the Catholic school Broeders Xaverianen and the neutral school Koninklijk Atheneum joined together to form the Brugsche Football Club. The former students christened the club's founding by establishing the Latin motto '[mens sana in corpore sano](/wiki/Mens_sana_in_corpore_sano "Mens sana in corpore sano")' (a healthy mind in a healthy body).[[9]](#cite_note-UEFAHistory-10) A year later on 13 November 1891, the club was re-created under Brugsche FC, and this is now seen as the official foundation of the current Club Brugge. In 1892, an official board was installed at the club to oversee all operations and team decisions. In 1895, the national athletics sports union was founded, predecessor of the later national football association, under the name UBSSA (Union Belge des Sociétés de Sports Athlétiques); Brugsche FC was a founding member of the UBSSSA and as such took part in the first league campaign organized in Belgian football during the [1895–96 season](/wiki/1895%E2%80%9396_Belgian_First_Division "1895–96 Belgian First Division").[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHenshaw197975-11) Financial difficulties in the following year forced the club to leave the UBSSA, and soon after, Football Club Brugeois was formed by breakaway club members. The two sides were reunited in 1897 under the French name of Football Club Brugeois; they did not take on the Dutch title Club Brugge until 1972.[[9]](#cite_note-UEFAHistory-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/FCB_1919-20.jpg/250px-FCB_1919-20.jpg)](/wiki/File:FCB_1919-20.jpg)

Picture of the 1919–20 squad

In 1914, FC Brugeois reached their first [Belgian Cup](/wiki/Belgian_Cup "Belgian Cup") final, but lost 2–1 to [Union SG](/wiki/Royale_Union_Saint-Gilloise "Royale Union Saint-Gilloise"). Six years later, the club claimed their first trophy, by winning the [Belgian First Division](/wiki/Belgian_First_Division_A "Belgian First Division A") during the [1919–20 season](/wiki/1919%E2%80%9320_Belgian_First_Division "1919–20 Belgian First Division").[[11]](#cite_note-rsssf-12) They celebrated by changing their title to Royal FC Brugeois – with their regal status now reflected in their modern prefix KV, standing for Koninklijke Vereniging (royal club).[[9]](#cite_note-UEFAHistory-10) Only eight years later though, the club was relegated to the [Belgian Second Division](/wiki/Belgian_Second_Division "Belgian Second Division") for the first time in their history following a relegation play-off.[[11]](#cite_note-rsssf-12) Further lean times followed the relegation in 1928, as they spent much of the 1940s and 1950s in the second division of Belgian football.[[9]](#cite_note-UEFAHistory-10)

Following the [1958–59](/wiki/1958%E2%80%9359_in_Belgian_football "1958–59 in Belgian football") season, the club earned promotion back to the First Division and have not been relegated since.[[9]](#cite_note-UEFAHistory-10) The club were able to add to their trophy cabinet in 1968, winning the first of their record 11 [Belgian Cup](/wiki/Belgian_Cup "Belgian Cup") titles for the first time after defeating [Beerschot A.C.](/wiki/Beerschot_A.C. "Beerschot A.C.") 7–6 in a penalty-shootout after a 1–1 draw.

The club enjoyed their most success under legendary Austrian manager [Ernst Happel](/wiki/Ernst_Happel "Ernst Happel") as he led the club to three straight league championships from [1975–76](/wiki/1975%E2%80%9376_Belgian_First_Division "1975–76 Belgian First Division") to [1977–78](/wiki/1977%E2%80%9378_Belgian_First_Division "1977–78 Belgian First Division") and a Belgian Cup victory in 1976–77.[[12]](#cite_note-13) Happel also guided Club Brugge to their first European final, reaching the [1976 UEFA Cup Final](/wiki/1976_UEFA_Cup_Final "1976 UEFA Cup Final"). Over the two-legged final against English giants [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C."), Club Brugge fell 3–4 on aggregate.[[13]](#cite_note-wood-14) Two years later, Brugge again met Liverpool in a European final, this time in the [1978 European Cup Final](/wiki/1978_European_Cup_Final "1978 European Cup Final") at [Wembley](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium_(1923) "Wembley Stadium (1923)"), becoming the first Belgian club to reach the final of the competition. Brugge fell to a lone second-half goal from [Kenny Dalglish](/wiki/Kenny_Dalglish "Kenny Dalglish") as Liverpool won their second European Cup and third European trophy in succession.[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-line-ups-16) Following the cup final loss to Liverpool, Happel left Club Brugge and would lead [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_national_football_team "Netherlands national football team") later that summer to the final of the [1978 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1978_FIFA_World_Cup "1978 FIFA World Cup").[[16]](#cite_note-17)

On 25 November 1992, Brugge player [Daniel Amokachi](/wiki/Daniel_Amokachi "Daniel Amokachi") became the first goal scorer in the [Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League"). He scored in a 1–0 win over [CSKA Moscow](/wiki/PFC_CSKA_Moscow "PFC CSKA Moscow").[[17]](#cite_note-18)

On 20 May 2021, Brugge drew 3–3 with [rivals](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht%E2%80%93Club_Brugge_KV_rivalry "R.S.C. Anderlecht–Club Brugge KV rivalry") [Anderlecht](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht "R.S.C. Anderlecht") to win the [Belgian First Division A](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Belgian_First_Division_A "2020–21 Belgian First Division A") title for the fourth time in six years and 17th time overall.[[18]](#cite_note-clinch-19) It was the first time since 1973 that Club Brugge had been crowned champions at Anderlecht's ground and the first time since 1976–77 and 1977–78 that Brugge had won back-to-back league titles.[[19]](#cite_note-20) A year later, they would become champions for a third time in a row at Antwerp's ground, the first time since 1977–79.[[20]](#cite_note-21) They would go on and qualify for the [2022–23 UEFA Champions League knockout](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_UEFA_Champions_League_knockout_phase "2022–23 UEFA Champions League knockout phase") for the first time in the modern history, after losing only once and keeping 5 [clean sheets](/wiki/Clean_sheets "Clean sheets") in the group stage.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

## Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Club_Brugge_KV&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Stadium")]

Main articles: [Jan Breydel Stadium](/wiki/Jan_Breydel_Stadium "Jan Breydel Stadium") and [Belfius Basecamp](/wiki/Belfius_Basecamp "Belfius Basecamp")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Panoramio_-_V%26A_Dudush_-_Jan_Breydel_Stadion.jpg/250px-Panoramio_-_V%26A_Dudush_-_Jan_Breydel_Stadion.jpg)](/wiki/File:Panoramio_-_V%26A_Dudush_-_Jan_Breydel_Stadion.jpg)

[Jan Breydel Stadium](/wiki/Jan_Breydel_Stadium "Jan Breydel Stadium")

The club's original home in the Sint-Andries district of Bruges was known as the Rattenplein (rats' stadium) since it was owned by the local fox terrier club, who used it for another imported English pastime: rat baiting.[[28]](#cite_note-rattenplein-29)[[9]](#cite_note-UEFAHistory-10) This non-UEFA affiliated 'sport' involved getting dogs to chase and kill rats.[[28]](#cite_note-rattenplein-29)[[9]](#cite_note-UEFAHistory-10) In 1911, the team moved to a new ground, called De Klokke (after a nearby pub), which was renamed the Albert Dyserynckstadion after the sudden death of Club Brugge chairman Albert Dyserynck.[[9]](#cite_note-UEFAHistory-10)

Their current stadium, since 1975, was rebranded in honour of local butcher and revolutionary [Jan Breydel](/wiki/Jan_Breydel "Jan Breydel") in 1998.[[9]](#cite_note-UEFAHistory-10) Breydel led a rising against the city's French overlords in the 1300s.[[9]](#cite_note-UEFAHistory-10) The venue – which Club Brugge share with local rivals [Cercle Brugge](/wiki/Cercle_Brugge_K.S.V. "Cercle Brugge K.S.V.") – was previously named the Olympiastadion.[[9]](#cite_note-UEFAHistory-10)[[29]](#cite_note-30)

In November 2016, the club broke ground on a [new training complex](/wiki/Belfius_Basecamp "Belfius Basecamp") at [Westkapelle](/wiki/Westkapelle,_Belgium "Westkapelle, Belgium"), including four training pitches and an additional training centre for the senior squad plus the U21 and U19 teams; all in addition to the already available sports complex Molenhoek.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

### New stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Club_Brugge_KV&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: New stadium")]

Since 2007, Club Brugge has been working on developing a new stadium. Since then, there have been a number of proposed locations, but the project never really took off due to problems with ground availability and endangered animal species on the proposed grounds.

However, when a new city council and mayor were sworn into office in the city of Bruges, the project went through a rebirth. Instead of moving out of the current Jan Breydelstadium, the site on which this stadium is built will be completely reconstructed into a park with a brand new stadium next to where the current stadium is situated. Although this project has been criticised by some, it's the furthest the club has come with a project. In October 2021 the club received their building permit. The club, the city and the Flemish government aim to have a functioning stadium by mid-2023, which will hold up to 40,116 spectators.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

## Supporters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Club_Brugge_KV&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Supporters")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Clubbruggetifo.jpg/250px-Clubbruggetifo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clubbruggetifo.jpg)

[Tifo](/wiki/Tifo "Tifo") before the Champions League game Club Brugge-[Rapid Wien](/wiki/Rapid_Wien "Rapid Wien") in 2005

Some of the fans are part of 62 supporter clubs in Belgium, which have more than 10,000 members. The "Supportersfederatie Club Brugge KV", founded in 1967, is recognized as the official supporters club of Club Brugge. The federation is made up of 60 recognized supporters' clubs and has an elected board to steer the operation in the right direction. Club Brugge is the most popular club in the Flanders region of Belgium.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

In tribute to the fans, often dubbed the [twelfth man](/wiki/12th_man_(football) "12th man (football)") in football, Club Brugge no longer assigns the number 12 to players. Club Brugge also has a TV show, CLUBtv, on the [Telenet](/wiki/Telenet_(Belgium) "Telenet (Belgium)") network since 21 July 2006.[[33]](#cite_note-34) This twice weekly show features exclusive interviews with players, coaches and managers.

## Mascot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Club_Brugge_KV&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Mascot")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Brugse_beren.jpg/250px-Brugse_beren.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brugse_beren.jpg)

The three Bears; mascots of Club Bruges

The official mascot of Club Bruges is a bear, symbol of the city of [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges"). The history of the bear is related to a legend of the first [Count of Flanders](/wiki/Count_of_Flanders "Count of Flanders"), [Baldwin I of Flanders](/wiki/Baldwin_I,_Margrave_of_Flanders "Baldwin I, Margrave of Flanders"), who had fought and defeated a bear in his youth. Since the end of 2000, a second mascot, also a bear, travels along the edge of the field during home games for fans to call and encourage both their favorites. These two bears are called Belle and Bene. In 2010, a third bear named Bibi, made its appearance. He is described as the child of the first two mascots, and is oriented towards the young supporters.

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Club_Brugge_KV&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

Main articles: [Bruges derby](/wiki/Bruges_derby "Bruges derby") and [R.S.C. Anderlecht–Club Brugge KV rivalry](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht%E2%80%93Club_Brugge_KV_rivalry "R.S.C. Anderlecht–Club Brugge KV rivalry")

Like many historic clubs, Club Brugge contests rivalries with other Belgian clubs, whether at local ([Cercle Brugge](/wiki/Cercle_Brugge_K.S.V. "Cercle Brugge K.S.V.")) or regional level ([Antwerp](/wiki/Royal_Antwerp_F.C. "Royal Antwerp F.C.")) or nationally competitive ([Anderlecht](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht "R.S.C. Anderlecht") and [Standard Liège](/wiki/Standard_Li%C3%A8ge "Standard Liège")).

### Anderlecht

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Club_Brugge_KV&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Anderlecht")]

The rivalry between Club Brugge and [Anderlecht](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht "R.S.C. Anderlecht") has developed since the 1970s. At that time, the [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels")-based club and Club Brugge won most trophies between them, leaving little room for other Belgian teams. Matches between these two teams were often contested for the title of champion of Belgium. Three Belgian Cup finals were played between the two clubs (with Anderlecht winning once and Club Brugge twice), and they played seven Belgian Supercups (Club Bruges won five). A match between these two sides is often called 'The Hate Game'. They are arguably the most heated fixtures in Belgian football together with clashes between the other two members of the [Big Three](/wiki/Big_Three_(Belgium) "Big Three (Belgium)") – Anderlecht and [Standard Liège](/wiki/Standard_Li%C3%A8ge "Standard Liège").[[34]](#cite_note-35)[[35]](#cite_note-36)

### Cercle Brugge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Club_Brugge_KV&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Cercle Brugge")]

The [Bruges Derby](/wiki/Bruges_derby "Bruges derby") is seen as one of the most important games of the season for a lot of fans from both teams. Every season, the game attracts a huge deal of fans which results in huge choreographies on both sides. [Tifos](/wiki/Tifo "Tifo"), flags and banners made specifically for this confrontation and accompanied by [flares](/wiki/Flare "Flare") and smoke bombs aren't a rare sight in and around the stadium. The winner of this derby is crowned "de Ploeg van Brugge*",* which translates to "the team of Bruges". It has become a tradition for the winning side to plant a flag with the club's crest or colours on the center spot after the game.

### R. Antwerp FC

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Club_Brugge_KV&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: R. Antwerp FC")]

The rivalry between the oldest clubs in Flanders and Belgium, is one that dates back to the 1900s. In 1908, due to Bruges supporters attacking Antwerp players after they had lost 2–1 to what we'll later call Club Brugge, one of the biggest and fiercest rivalries in Europe came to be.[[36]](#cite_note-37) Confrontations between the two sides bring a lot of fighting and havoc to the stadium and the surrounding neighbourhoods. This hatred has reached new highs ever since Antwerp gained promotion back to the first division.[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40)
